text: Bone stress injury occurs along a continuum from stress reaction to stress fracture to complete bone fracture, resulting from the inability of bone to withstand repetitive loading.
Most bone stress injuries heal following a period of modified loading and a progressive return to running activities.
Bone stress injuries have a high recurrence rate, signaling a need to address underlying causative factors rather than only treating the acute injury.
Increasing stride rate (cadence) can reduce impact-related forces on bone and lower the risk of bone stress injuries.
Calf strengthening is recommended for preventing tibial bone stress injuries, while foot intrinsic strengthening targets metatarsal bone stress injuries.
Instructing runners to run 'softer' (reducing ground reaction forces) is a modifiable factor for reducing bone stress injury risk.
Malalignments and abnormal movement patterns should be assessed and corrected as part of bone stress injury prevention in runners.
